title: Inside NDC: The Quiet Rewrite of How Airlines Sell Seats
dek: New Distribution Capability is reshaping how Asian and global carriers package fares, bags and extras. As airlines shift to an offers-and-orders model, here is what NDC means for travel agents, corporate buyers and the price you ultimately pay.
body:
A standard you will rarely see on a boarding pass is steadily changing how airlines sell. New Distribution Capability, the IATA-backed data standard known as NDC, lets carriers build and distribute richer, more dynamic offers across booking channels rather than relying solely on the legacy fare-filing systems that underpinned ticketing for decades. By 2026 dozens of airlines worldwide, including major Asian and Gulf carriers, had adopted NDC in some form as the industry pushes toward a modern "offers and orders" model.

From fare codes to dynamic offers

Traditional airline distribution leaned on fixed fare classes and intermediaries that often presented little more than a price and a basic fare type. NDC, by contrast, is an XML-based standard that lets an airline package the price together with product attributes, such as seat type, baggage allowance, Wi-Fi, lounge access or meals, into a single tailored offer that can reach travel agents, corporate booking tools and online sellers directly.

IATA frames this as a broader shift to "offers and orders," in which the airline retakes control of how its products are created and bundled, and tracks the booking as a single order rather than a tangle of separate records. To gauge progress, the industry body maintains an Airline Maturity Index recognising carriers that have implemented the standard.

What it means for buyers and the price you pay

For corporate travel programmes and agencies, NDC promises clearer comparisons of what each fare actually includes, plus access to content and bundles that may not surface through older channels. Industry analyses have suggested modern airline retailing could unlock tens of billions of dollars in additional value across the sector over the coming years, though such projections are estimates rather than guarantees.

  • Offers can bundle fare, bags and extras instead of a bare ticket price.
  • Airlines gain finer control over pricing and how products are presented.
  • Agents and corporate tools can surface richer, comparable fare content.
  • Adoption remains uneven, and integration across systems is still maturing.

The transition is far from finished. Travel sellers must invest in new technology, and content can still differ by channel during the changeover. For passengers, NDC is largely invisible, but it increasingly shapes which offers appear, how they are compared and how easily extras can be added, making it one of the most consequential behind-the-scenes shifts in commercial aviation.
